case: 24x24 front-facing goblin head (complex, symmetry-heavy; INDEPENDENT BLIND JUDGE PANEL)
step_toggled: the live_save_preview see-step in the draw -> see -> fix loop
method: Drew a 24x24 goblin head as a blind first pass (no preview) = 'without' (cand_A). Then applied the perception loop on the same base: live_save_preview revealed (1) the right eye drawn a row LOWER than the left (vertical asymmetry) and (2) flat skin; fixed the eye alignment and added a hue-shifted directional ramp (light forehead / shadow jaw+right) = 'with' (cand_B). The two CLEAN renders were scored BLIND by an independent 3-judge panel (workflow subagents) on the rules/06 rubric, with scrambled labels and no knowledge of which used perception.

blind_judge_panel.delta_form_light: +3.33 (3.0 -> 6.33) -- the dominant gain: flat -> hue-shifted directional ramp

judge_observations_blind: All 3 judges independently picked the perception version and, without being told the hypothesis, named exactly what the see-step fixed: the value/hue ramp + light direction (vs flat), and better-matched eyes (they flagged the without-version's eyes as mismatched). Silhouette + linework tied (same base shape + outline; no orphans either way).
verdict: On a complex sprite the see-step measurably improved the result: 3/3 blind judges preferred it, mean overall +1.33, driven by form/light +3.33 and the eye-asymmetry fix. This is the error-catching + polish value the simple-sword run (Run 2) could not exercise.
caveats: Still N=1 case (one sprite); the panel is 3 LLM judges (consistent, but model-correlated).
The perception variant is the blind base + fixes, so the delta isolates the see-step's value (not a different drawing).
The blind first pass deliberately reflects a no-feedback draw (a vertical eye slip + flat fill), which is what an agent without the see-step produces.
